briefing.opening: Good day. We will now begin the climate briefing for July 3, 2026. Today's briefing primarily focused on warnings regarding the intensifying global climate crisis, alongside updates on carbon neutrality efforts and political barriers both domestically and internationally.

sections.title: 🔴 Intensifying Global Climate Crisis and Urgent Warnings
sections.content: Europe is suffering from extreme heatwaves, to the extent that people must endure them without air conditioning [3], and 2,000 deaths occurred in Spain and France due to the June heat [55]. Scientists warn that heatwaves caused by climate change will place a significant burden on power grids, and 'high-temperature damage' to plant leaves will increase due to global warming [49, 51]. Research indicating that even birds in protected areas of Ontario, Canada, cannot escape the effects of climate change demonstrates the vulnerability of entire ecosystems [31], and Caritas warns that climate change could displace millions of people [39]. Industrial emissions and pollution have been identified as the primary causes of climate change in the Louisiana region, and methane is referred to as a 'climate crime suspect' caused by human greed and negligence, emphasizing the need for immediate action [4, 36].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Acceleration of Domestic Carbon Neutrality and Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: Domestically, efforts to achieve carbon neutrality are expanding in various fields, including policy, education, and technology development. The Seoul Metropolitan Government is shifting the priority of its urban agriculture policy to climate crisis response and carbon neutrality [2, 5, 6, 18], and Incheon National University has fully launched carbon neutrality practice education to create a green campus [7, 12, 15]. Gyeongsangbuk-do plans to secure a carbon neutrality R&D budget of 222.7 billion KRW to foster Pohang as a climate tech hub [13], and the realization of carbon neutrality in the truck transport sector through the introduction of next-generation biodiesel fuel is also gaining momentum [10]. Furthermore, Korea Southern Power (KOSPO) and Korea Western Power (KOWEPO) are accelerating the energy transition by operating renewable energy business communication platforms and expanding offshore wind power projects [19, 21, 25], and new carbon neutrality and clean energy technologies are expected to be showcased at '2026 Green Enertech' [14].

sections.title: ⚠️ Political Barriers and Controversies Hindering Climate Action
sections.content: Political and policy barriers hindering climate change response still exist. A U.S. appeals court allowed the Trump administration's decision to remove 'slavery and climate change exhibits' to stand, which aligns with attempts to delete climate change information [1, 54, 60]. Scientific figures like Bill Nye warn that concerns about heatwaves and climate change during the Trump administration were 'very, very serious,' and criticism continues regarding attitudes that deny the dangers of climate change [37, 42]. Domestically, controversy has also arisen regarding the stability of renewable energy; Representative Koo Ja-keun pointed out that even with the use of ESS (Energy Storage Systems), solar power generation during late-night hours becomes '0,' calling the claim to supply power to semiconductor complexes with renewable energy absurd [22, 28]. Such political decisions and skepticism can undermine the urgency of climate change response.

sections.title: 🌍 Expansion of International Cooperation and Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: In the international community, various efforts are underway for climate change adaptation and resolution. In Sweden, an innovative initiative allows young people to participate in climate change response activities and receive salaries [29, 59], and research assisting local councils in climate change management is also being actively utilized [47]. The UN General Assembly has adopted a groundbreaking International Court of Justice (ICJ) opinion, raising expectations for strengthened environmental action [48], thus establishing a foundation for climate crisis response at the international legal level. Furthermore, important insights into climate adaptation and resilience were presented during London Climate Action Week [50], and scientific exploration efforts to deeply understand the impacts of climate change are steadily continuing, such as a German icebreaker heading to an Arctic observatory to track 25 years of climate change [45, 61].

briefing.closing: In today's briefing, we examined the escalating climate crisis, the barriers hindering action, and simultaneously, the active carbon neutrality and adaptation efforts underway both domestically and internationally. Climate change is progressing at an unprecedented pace, moving beyond scientific warnings to actual damage. In this complex situation, only the active participation and tangible actions of national governments, local communities, and individuals can pave the way for a sustainable future. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: In latest legal twist, appeals panel rules US does not have to reinstate signage on climate, immigration and slaveryThe Trump administration does not have to reinstate materials related to climate change, immigration and slavery that it has removed from national parks, a US appeals court ruled on Thursday.It’s the latest twist in a legal battle over how history is remembered at American public monuments. articles.title: Voyage to the end of the world: floating lab to explore life in Arctic adrift in ice
articles.description: An eight-month expedition will set off soon from Norway on a mission to find new species before the climate crisis and pollution changes the northern ocean for everSix scientists and six crew will travel next month to Kirkenes, a remote Arctic town in Norway near the Russian border, to begin an odyssey to one of the most inhospitable, inaccessible and least-studied regions on Earth. There, they will climb onboard a futuristic, floating laboratory – the French-built Tara polar station.They will e

summary.phenomenon: Next month, six scientists and six crew members will embark on an eight-month expedition from Kirkenes, Norway, aboard a futuristic floating laboratory called the French-built Tara Polar Station. They will explore the Arctic Ocean, one of the least-studied regions on Earth.
summary.cause: This expedition is being undertaken with the purpose of discovering and studying new species before the climate crisis and pollution permanently alter the Arctic Ocean.
summary.outlook: The expedition aims to search for Arctic life drifting within the ice and to discover and document new species before the Arctic Ocean is permanently changed by the climate crisis and pollution.
